content.text: Containers package an application and its dependencies into a portable, self-contained unit that runs identically across environments. When AI agents execute code, containerization provides a principled boundary between the agent-generated logic and the host system — making every execution reproducible, auditable, and hardened against a wide class of malicious or buggy code behaviors.

Containerization security refers to the set of mechanisms, policies, and practices that constrain what a containerized process can do on the host system. A container is not a virtual machine — it shares the host OS kernel — so the security boundary must be enforced through a combination of kernel features: Linux namespaces for resource partitioning, control groups (cgroups) for resource limits, seccomp for syscall filtering, Linux capabilities for privilege reduction, and mandatory access control (MAC) frameworks such as AppArmor or SELinux for path- and action-level policies.

In the context of agentic AI, containerization security is applied specifically at the code-execution layer. An AI orchestrator generates code snippets — Python scripts, shell commands, data analysis pipelines — and submits them for execution. The container wraps that execution in an environment where only a precisely enumerated set of operations is permitted. Because each container is ephemeral (created fresh, destroyed after the run), state contamination between agent turns is eliminated by construction.

The Open Container Initiative (OCI) standardizes the image format and runtime interface, which means security hardening techniques developed for one OCI-compliant runtime — Docker, containerd, Podman — apply uniformly across tools. The
low-level runtime is the reference implementation that actually invokes the kernel primitives; higher-level runtimes delegate to it (or to alternatives like

When an AI agent executes code, it operates with the same OS-level privileges as the process that invoked it — unless an explicit isolation boundary is interposed. Without containerization, a prompt-injected instruction to delete files, a carelessly generated
call, or a malicious pip package installed during a code-generation step can interact directly with the host filesystem, internal network, and credentials store.

— an agent reading a document or webpage can encounter embedded instructions to exfiltrate API keys; containerization with network isolation blocks the exfiltration path even when the injection succeeds.
can pull in packages with supply-chain malware; read-only root filesystems and network restrictions limit what those packages can do.
— an infinite loop or a deliberately crafted memory-exhaustion payload in agent output can crash the host without cgroup limits; containers make resource DoS a recoverable, per-execution event.
— code executing on a host with internal service access can pivot laterally; per-container network namespaces with egress filtering remove this pivot surface.
— without ephemeral containers, a previous run's temporary files, environment variables, or cached credentials can influence subsequent runs, creating unpredictable and exploitable behavior.

— every run starts from an identical image; there is no dependency on host-installed packages, accumulated state, or environment drift between runs. This makes agent behavior reproducible and debugging tractable.
Defense in depth against model failures

— even when the language model generates malicious, buggy, or prompt-injected code, the container limits the blast radius to the ephemeral environment. The host system, secrets, and other users' data remain unexposed.

command with explicit flags, a seccomp JSON profile, and a cgroup configuration constitute a version-controllable security contract. It can be code-reviewed, tested, and diffed alongside application code.
— per-container CPU, memory, and PID limits ensure that one user's expensive computation does not starve others on shared infrastructure.

— containerized, network-isolated execution is a prerequisite for processing regulated data under SOC 2, ISO 27001, and HIPAA, where agent-generated code must not be able to access PII or PHI at rest on the host.
— OCI-standard images and runtime spec files work identically across cloud providers, Kubernetes clusters, and on-premise bare metal, so hardening done in development applies directly to production.
Shared host kernel is the fundamental limitation

— all containers on a host share the same Linux kernel. A kernel-level exploit (e.g., a namespace escape or a seccomp bypass via an unpatched CVE) can grant the attacker host-level access regardless of the container policy. MicroVM isolation (Firecracker, gVisor) addresses this at the cost of additional latency and operational complexity.

— launching a fresh container takes 100–500 ms on a warm Docker daemon with the image already cached. For interactive agentic use cases with a target response time under 2 seconds, this overhead is significant and must be managed with container pre-warming pools.
Policy misconfiguration is silent and dangerous

— an overly permissive seccomp profile, a missing capability drop, or accidental volume mount of the Docker socket provides a false sense of security. The policy must be audited as rigorously as application code.
— a compromised base image in the registry introduces vulnerabilities before the container even runs. Images must be signed, scanned for CVEs, and pulled from private or verified registries.

— containerization controls what code can do during execution, but it does not sanitize what the code writes to stdout. Arbitrarily long or injected outputs can still influence the next LLM call if stdout is not length-capped and treated as untrusted.
— mounting host directories read-only into the container still exposes those files to the process. If the agent code is buggy, it may leak file contents to stdout even without network access.

Containerization security is not a single mechanism but a stack of independent kernel and runtime controls that each address a different threat axis. Understanding each layer separately is important because a gap in one layer is not automatically covered by the others.

Linux namespaces partition kernel data structures so that a process inside a namespace sees its own isolated view of a resource. The PID namespace gives the container process a private process tree where its init is PID 1 and it cannot see host processes. The network namespace provides an isolated network stack — its own interfaces, routing table, and iptables rules — meaning that by default the container has no route to the host or any external network. The mount namespace controls which filesystem paths are visible, enabling a read-only root with a separate writable
. The user namespace maps UID 0 inside the container to an unprivileged UID on the host, so even apparent root inside the container has no host privileges.
cgroups v2 impose hard resource ceilings on the process tree inside the container.
caps the CPU bandwidth as a fraction of available time.
triggers an OOM kill when the process hierarchy exceeds the memory ceiling, preventing host memory exhaustion.
caps the number of processes/threads, defeating fork bombs.
limits block device I/O bandwidth. These limits are enforced by the kernel scheduler and I/O subsystem — they cannot be bypassed from user space regardless of how the code attempts to do so.

Seccomp (secure computing mode) attaches a BPF program to the process that is evaluated on every system call before the kernel processes it. If the call number or its arguments do not match the allowlist, the kernel either kills the process (
). A well-designed sandbox allows read/write on pre-opened file descriptors, memory allocation, clock access, and safe exits — while denying
with dangerous flags. Because the filter runs at the syscall boundary, user-space techniques cannot bypass it.
Linux capabilities decompose the traditional all-or-nothing root privilege into about 40 distinct capabilities. Dropping all capabilities with

removes the ability to bind low ports, mount filesystems, load kernel modules, change file ownership, and many other privileged operations. Containers running agent-generated code need no capabilities at all for pure computation — they only need the basic POSIX file and process operations that unprivileged users have anyway.
ensures that no child process can regain capabilities through setuid binaries.
MAC frameworks add a policy layer above POSIX permissions that cannot be overridden even by root. Docker's default AppArmor profile (
, and other dangerous host-visible paths. A custom profile for agent code execution can additionally deny write access to all paths outside
, deny network operations entirely, and write audit log entries for every policy violation — giving operators visibility into what the agent code attempted.

Container images use a union filesystem (OverlayFS on modern Linux) to stack read-only image layers beneath a single writable container layer. For agent code execution the writable layer is replaced by an in-memory
and the root filesystem is mounted read-only with

. This means agent code cannot modify the image, cannot persist files across runs, and cannot accumulate state that influences future executions. The tmpfs capacity limit enforces a ceiling on how much output data the code can generate before the run is killed.

attrs.alt: Containerization security architecture showing the AI orchestrator sending code through the OCI runtime and security enforcement layers (namespaces, seccomp, cgroups, AppArmor) into an ephemeral container, with escape attempts blocked and only sanitized stdout returned.

content.text: The diagram shows the security stack from top to bottom: the orchestrator submits a run request through the OCI runtime, which applies the full enforcement stack before the code process starts. Inside the ephemeral container, image layers are read-only; only a size-limited tmpfs is writable. The only output path is captured stdout flowing back up to the orchestrator — all other interactions with the host (filesystem reads, network connections, privilege escalations) are intercepted at the enforcement layer.

Think of a container sandbox as a sealed examination room. The AI agent (invigilator) passes an exam paper (code) through a slot in the wall. Inside the room, a student (the container process) works on the exam using only the tools pre-loaded in the room — no phone, no textbook, no external contact. When finished, the student passes the answer sheet back through the slot. The room itself is destroyed and rebuilt from scratch for the next student.

The security properties follow naturally from this model: the room's contents are determined before the exam starts (the image), the room cannot be modified during the exam (read-only root), the student cannot communicate with the outside world (network isolation), and the only thing that leaves is the answer sheet (stdout), which the invigilator checks before acting on it. This model also makes the attack surface clear: the only leverage an adversarial exam paper has on the outside world is what it writes in the answers.
Any agent with a code-execution tool
— the moment an agent can run Python, JavaScript, or shell, every invocation must be containerized. There is no secure alternative for production deployments.
Multi-agent pipelines where one agent evaluates code from another
— when an orchestrator agent passes code to a sub-agent for evaluation, the evaluation step must be containerized regardless of the apparent trust level of the generating agent.
Data analysis over regulated or sensitive data
— agent-generated pandas or SQL scripts processing PII must run in network-isolated containers so they cannot exfiltrate the data even if compromised.
— agent-generated build scripts, test generation, or deployment automation must be sandboxed to prevent access to production credentials and infrastructure.
When NOT to use it at the inference layer

— model inference (calling the LLM API) does not benefit from containerization in most architectures. The boundary is at code execution, not at text generation. Containerize the tool-call executor, not the LLM client.
When NOT to use full containers for purely in-process tools

— lightweight tools that only manipulate in-memory data structures (e.g., a JSON formatter) and make no OS calls do not need container isolation. The overhead is not justified for purely functional transforms.
The following steps describe building a hardened container-based sandbox for AI agent code execution using Docker. The same options apply to Podman (rootless) and containerd via
. Remove package managers, shells, and build tools with a
directive so the process does not start as root.
Start from Docker's default profile and remove syscalls not used by the runtime. Deny
explicitly. Test by running typical agent workloads and checking for seccomp violations in kernel logs.
Set resource limits on every run.
. Do not rely on defaults — they are unlimited on many Docker installations.
for any temporary file writes the code needs.
Drop all capabilities and block privilege escalation.
that the effective capability set is empty.
unless the code legitimately requires network access, in which case use a dedicated restricted bridge network with an explicit egress allowlist enforced by iptables or a cloud security group.
call in an orchestrator-level timeout. After the deadline, call
Sanitize and cap stdout before returning to the agent.
Truncate output at a fixed byte limit (e.g., 16 KB), validate UTF-8 encoding, strip ANSI escape sequences, and treat the result as untrusted content when constructing the next LLM prompt.

# Minimal Python sandbox image for AI agent code execution
FROM python:3.12-alpine AS base

# Install only runtime dependencies; remove package manager after
RUN pip install --no-cache-dir numpy pandas && \
    pip cache purge

# Remove apk to prevent package installation during execution
RUN apk del apk-tools busybox wget curl || true

# Remove shells that could be used for command execution
RUN rm -f /bin/sh /bin/ash /usr/bin/env

# Create a non-root user for running agent code
RUN addgroup -S sandbox && adduser -S -G sandbox -u 1000 sandbox

# Set working directory accessible to sandbox user
RUN mkdir /workspace && chown sandbox:sandbox /workspace
WORKDIR /workspace

USER sandbox

# No CMD — the orchestrator passes code via volume mount
Example 1 — Minimal hardened docker run for agent code

The following command executes agent-generated Python code from a read-only bind mount with the full set of security flags applied. The code is passed as a file (not a command-line argument) to avoid shell injection through argument escaping.

#!/usr/bin/env bash
# execute_agent_code.sh <script_path>
# Runs an agent-generated Python script inside a hardened container.

SCRIPT="$(realpath "$1")"
TIMEOUT=20

docker run \
  --rm \
  --name "agent-sandbox-$$" \
  --memory="128m" \
  --memory-swap="128m" \
  --cpus="0.5" \
  --pids-limit=32 \
  --network=none \
  --read-only \
  --tmpfs /tmp:size=32m,noexec,nosuid \
  --cap-drop=ALL \
  --security-opt no-new-privileges:true \
  --security-opt seccomp=/etc/docker/seccomp/agent-sandbox.json \
  --user 1000:1000 \
  --volume "${SCRIPT}:/workspace/run.py:ro" \
  agent-sandbox:latest \
  timeout "${TIMEOUT}" python /workspace/run.py 2>/dev/null

# Non-zero exit code from timeout(1) means the process was killed.
Example 2 — Python SDK wrapper with resource enforcement and output caps

Orchestrators typically call the Docker SDK from Python rather than shelling out. The following wrapper handles timeouts, truncates output, and ensures the container is always removed even when the execution raises an exception.

import docker
import tempfile
import os
import re

MEMORY_LIMIT = "128m"
CPU_LIMIT = 0.5          # fraction of one CPU core
PIDS_LIMIT = 32
TIMEOUT_SEC = 20
MAX_OUTPUT_BYTES = 16_384  # 16 KB
SECCOMP_PROFILE = "/etc/docker/seccomp/agent-sandbox.json"

def run_agent_code(code: str) -> dict:
    """
    Execute agent-generated Python code in a hardened container.

    Returns:
        {"stdout": str, "exit_code": int, "timed_out": bool, "error": str | None}
    """
    client = docker.from_env()

    # Write agent code to a temp file to avoid command-line injection
    with tempfile.NamedTemporaryFile(
        mode="w", suffix=".py", dir="/tmp", delete=False
    ) as fh:
        fh.write(code)
        script_path = fh.name

    container = None
    try:
        container = client.containers.run(
            image="agent-sandbox:latest",
            command=["python", "/workspace/run.py"],
            volumes={script_path: {"bind": "/workspace/run.py", "mode": "ro"}},
            detach=True,
            # Resource limits
            mem_limit=MEMORY_LIMIT,
            memswap_limit=MEMORY_LIMIT,  # disable swap
            nano_cpus=int(CPU_LIMIT * 1e9),
            pids_limit=PIDS_LIMIT,
            # Isolation
            network_mode="none",
            read_only=True,
            tmpfs={"/tmp": "size=32m,noexec,nosuid"},
            cap_drop=["ALL"],
            security_opt=[
                "no-new-privileges:true",
                f"seccomp={SECCOMP_PROFILE}",
            ],
            user="1000:1000",
        )

        timed_out = False
        try:
            container.wait(timeout=TIMEOUT_SEC)
        except Exception:
            timed_out = True
            container.kill()

        raw = container.logs(stdout=True, stderr=False)
        stdout = raw[:MAX_OUTPUT_BYTES].decode("utf-8", errors="replace")
        # Strip ANSI escape codes to prevent terminal injection
        stdout = re.sub(r"\x1b\[[0-9;]*[mGKHF]", "", stdout)

        state = container.attrs.get("State", {}) if not timed_out else {}
        exit_code = state.get("ExitCode", -1) if not timed_out else -1

        return {"stdout": stdout, "exit_code": exit_code,
                "timed_out": timed_out, "error": None}

    except Exception as exc:
        return {"stdout": "", "exit_code": -1,
                "timed_out": False, "error": str(exc)}
    finally:
        if container:
            container.remove(force=True)
        os.unlink(script_path)
Example 3 — Custom seccomp profile for a Python-only sandbox

A seccomp allowlist that covers only the system calls a Python 3.12 process makes during pure computation — no subprocess spawning, no sockets, no filesystem writes outside pre-opened file descriptors.
(no privilege change). Any attempt to call these returns EPERM, which Python converts to an

A healthcare analytics platform deploys an AI assistant that generates custom SQL and Python analysis scripts on demand, running them against de-identified patient datasets. Patient data is loaded into a read-only PostgreSQL container accessible only within an isolated Docker bridge network. Agent-generated Python scripts that query the database run in a separate analysis container on the same bridge, with egress rules permitting only connections to the PostgreSQL container on port 5432 — all other outbound connections are dropped by iptables. Each analysis container is torn down after the query completes; no state persists between sessions.

import docker
import json
import tempfile
import os

ANALYSIS_IMAGE = "health-analytics-sandbox:2.1.0"
DB_NETWORK = "analytics-internal"   # restricted bridge; only port 5432 egress
MAX_MEMORY = "512m"
MAX_CPU = 1.0
TIMEOUT_SEC = 60

def run_health_analysis(agent_sql_script: str) -> dict:
    """
    Execute an agent-generated analytics script against the de-identified
    patient database. Network restricted to internal analytics bridge only.
    """
    client = docker.from_env()

    with tempfile.NamedTemporaryFile(
        mode="w", suffix=".py", delete=False
    ) as fh:
        fh.write(agent_sql_script)
        script_path = fh.name

    container = None
    try:
        container = client.containers.run(
            image=ANALYSIS_IMAGE,
            command=["python", "/analysis/script.py"],
            volumes={script_path: {"bind": "/analysis/script.py", "mode": "ro"}},
            detach=True,
            # Resource limits
            mem_limit=MAX_MEMORY,
            memswap_limit=MAX_MEMORY,
            nano_cpus=int(MAX_CPU * 1e9),
            pids_limit=64,
            # Network: internal only — PostgreSQL reachable, internet blocked
            network=DB_NETWORK,
            # Filesystem isolation
            read_only=True,
            tmpfs={"/tmp": "size=64m,noexec"},
            # Privilege restrictions
            cap_drop=["ALL"],
            security_opt=["no-new-privileges:true"],
            user="1000:1000",
            # Environment: only the DB URL, no credentials for other services
            environment={"DB_URL": "postgresql://readonly@analytics-db:5432/deidentified"},
        )

        timed_out = False
        try:
            container.wait(timeout=TIMEOUT_SEC)
        except Exception:
            timed_out = True
            container.kill()

        output = container.logs(stdout=True, stderr=False)[:32768]
        result = json.loads(output.decode("utf-8")) if not timed_out else {}
        return {"data": result, "timed_out": timed_out}

    finally:
        if container:
            container.remove(force=True)
        os.unlink(script_path)

This pattern ensures that even if the agent generates a script that attempts to call external APIs, POST data to an attacker server, or read host files, every such attempt is blocked at the network or filesystem layer before it reaches the OS. The only observable side effect of the container run is the JSON written to stdout.
Running the sandbox container as root
— the default UID in many base images is 0. Without an explicit
directive in the image), a kernel escape hands the attacker root on the host.

inside a container gives that container the ability to create new privileged containers, effectively granting host root. This is an unconditional sandbox escape and must never appear in a sandbox specification.
grants the container all capabilities and disables the seccomp profile, effectively removing the entire security boundary. It is for container management tooling, not agent sandboxes.
Passing agent code via environment variables
to all processes in the container and can be logged by some runtimes. Always pass code via a read-only file mount or stdin.
, stopped containers accumulate on the host, consuming disk space through their writable layers and creating a forensic record of every run that may contain sensitive data.

— without a byte limit on captured output, a sandbox that returns unlimited data allows code to write hundreds of megabytes of content back to the orchestrator, wasting tokens or embedding large prompt-injection payloads that exceed the context window.
Using image tags instead of digests
by tag does not guarantee the same image across runs; a supply-chain attack that compromises the tag on Docker Hub will silently swap the base image. Pin with
— if the same container is reused, files written to
by one execution are visible to the next. Each agent code execution must use a fresh container.
Layer all five security mechanisms — no single layer is sufficient.

Namespaces partition resources; cgroups cap resource usage; seccomp restricts syscalls; capabilities limit privilege; MAC adds path-level control. Omitting any one layer creates a class of attacks not covered by the others.
A denylist must enumerate every dangerous syscall and will miss any new or unusual attack vector. An allowlist starts from zero and adds only what is verified to be needed by the specific runtime.
Pre-warm a pool of idle containers.

Cold container startup (including image layer mount, namespace setup, and cgroup creation) takes 100–500 ms. For interactive agent use cases with a 2-second response target, maintain a pool of 5–20 warm containers per worker and swap them out after each use, replenishing asynchronously.
Pin images by digest and scan for CVEs on every build.
Tools like Trivy, Snyk, or Grype can scan images in CI and block promotion of images with critical-severity CVEs. Pin the base image digest in the Dockerfile to guarantee reproducibility.
Log every execution with its full security context.

Append an audit record containing the container ID, the seccomp profile applied, the resource limits, the exit code, elapsed time, and the first 1 KB of stdout. This provides post-incident forensic evidence without storing sensitive full outputs.
Fail closed on security option rejection.
If the container runtime rejects the seccomp profile, refuses to apply a capability drop, or fails to mount the tmpfs, abort the execution entirely. Never fall back to an unsandboxed run.

Running the container runtime itself as a non-root user (Podman rootless, Docker rootless mode) means that even a complete container escape only grants unprivileged host access, dramatically limiting the damage of a kernel exploit.

The primary latency cost of containerized AI agent execution is container startup time. On a host with the image already pulled and cached in the layer store, creating a new container, applying seccomp and cgroup configuration, and reaching the point where the process is executing Python typically takes 100–300 ms with the standard
runtime. This is acceptable for background analysis workloads (batch jobs, async pipelines) but marginal for synchronous interactive agents where the user expects sub-second response times.

Pre-warmed container pools reduce the effective startup overhead to near zero from the agent's perspective. In this model, a pool manager creates a set of idle containers at startup, each already past the namespace/cgroup initialization phase and waiting for work. When the orchestrator needs a sandbox, it withdraws a container from the pool, injects the code via a Unix domain socket or a watch on a pre-mounted directory, captures the output, and returns the container (or, in the ephemeral model, destroys it and instructs the pool to replenish with a fresh one). Netflix's Titus, AWS Lambda (via Firecracker), and E2B all use variants of this warm-pool pattern.
Memory overhead per container slot depends on the base image and the workload. A

container consumes roughly 40–60 MB RSS before any user code runs. On a machine with 32 GB RAM, accounting for OS and orchestrator overhead, approximately 400–500 concurrent Python sandbox slots are theoretically possible — though in practice contention on the Docker daemon's gRPC endpoint becomes a bottleneck at around 100–200 concurrent creates/removes per second. Beyond that rate, use a higher-level scheduler (Kubernetes Jobs, a custom container pool daemon, or a managed service) to distribute work across multiple daemon endpoints.
Full seccomp, cgroup v2, AppArmor, rootless mode; widely supported
No daemon required; rootless by default; drops privileges at host level
Kubernetes-native; used by most managed container platforms; snapshotter plugins
Intercepts syscalls in Go user-space kernel; eliminates kernel-sharing risk
Scans image layers for known vulnerabilities in CI before promotion
Detects unexpected syscall patterns, /proc reads, network socket creation inside containers
Firecracker-backed sandboxes; SDK for Python/JS; designed for LLM code execution
Verifies that the image pulled at runtime matches the CI-built and signed artifact

OCI containers with the full security stack applied occupy the sweet spot for most production agentic AI deployments: they offer meaningful isolation, work with the existing ecosystem of tooling and managed platforms, and can be performance-tuned with pre-warming to approach the latency of bare subprocesses. Escalating to gVisor or Firecracker is warranted when the threat model includes adversarial code that actively attempts kernel exploits, or when regulatory requirements mandate VM-level isolation between tenant workloads.

The dominant residual risk in OCI container isolation is a kernel-level exploit. Because all containers on a host share the Linux kernel, a working exploit for a kernel namespace or seccomp bypass CVE can grant the attacker host-level privileges even in a well-configured sandbox. The Linux kernel releases approximately 200–400 CVEs per year; of these, a handful typically affect container isolation primitives. The practical mitigation strategy has three pillars: minimize exposure time through rapid kernel patching, increase isolation depth for high-risk workloads with gVisor or Firecracker, and detect anomalous behavior at runtime.

— the sandbox fleet's host OS must be patched within 24–72 hours of a kernel security release. Automate OS patching and schedule container host reboots during low-traffic windows. An unpatched kernel is the most common reason a theoretically strong container security posture is actually weak.
— Falco attaches an eBPF probe to the kernel and alerts on syscall patterns that indicate suspicious activity inside containers: unexpected
, socket creation in a no-network container, or access to

— use Cosign or Docker Content Trust to cryptographically sign images at build time and verify signatures before pulling. A compromised registry tag that bypasses signature verification is a supply-chain attack vector that bypasses all runtime security controls.
Graceful handling of OOM kills and timeouts

— the orchestrator must detect when a container exits due to OOM kill (exit code 137) or timeout and return a structured error to the agent rather than silently failing or retrying with larger resource limits. Automatic resource limit escalation on retry is a denial-of-service vector.
— validate that captured stdout is within the byte limit, is valid UTF-8, and does not contain known prompt-injection patterns (e.g.,
sequences) before inserting it into the next LLM context window. Treat container output as external, untrusted content regardless of what the code was supposed to do.
Defense in depth beyond the container

— containerization is one layer. It works alongside prompt-level guardrails (preventing the model from generating overtly malicious code), output classifiers (detecting injected instructions in stdout), and data access controls (read-only mounts with minimal data exposure). No single control is sufficient on its own.

Containerization security for AI agent code execution combines five independent kernel mechanisms: Linux namespaces (resource partitioning), cgroups v2 (resource limits), seccomp (syscall allowlisting), capabilities (privilege reduction), and MAC frameworks like AppArmor (path-level policy) — each layer independently limits a different class of attack.
The OCI standard (Docker, containerd, Podman) provides a portable, version-controllable security contract: the
flags, seccomp JSON profile, and cgroup settings that harden the sandbox are code-reviewable artifacts that travel with the deployment.
Every container running agent-generated code should use
, and an explicit seccomp profile. These are not optional hardening tweaks — they are the baseline security policy.

The shared host kernel is the fundamental limitation of OCI containers: a kernel CVE can enable escape even from a perfectly configured sandbox. For adversarial or compliance-critical workloads, escalate to gVisor (user-space kernel) or Firecracker (separate kernel per sandbox) to eliminate this shared surface.
Pre-warmed container pools are required for interactive agentic use cases — cold container startup of 100–300 ms makes per-request sandbox creation impractical at conversational response times.
Never mount the Docker socket, never use
, and never pass code via environment variables inside a sandbox container — these three anti-patterns each independently provide a path to full host compromise.

The output path (stdout returned to the orchestrator) is the primary remaining attack surface: length-cap it, validate encoding, strip terminal escape sequences, and treat it as untrusted external content before inserting it into any LLM prompt.

Container image supply-chain hygiene is as important as runtime security: pin base images by digest, sign images in CI with Cosign, scan for CVEs before promotion, and patch host kernels within 48 hours of security releases.
